core_meaning: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる covers a wide family of meanings centered on movement or change going 'upward' — physically rising, entering an enclosed space (like a house), prices or levels increasing, or a person finishing/completing something. It also commonly means to become nervous or flustered in performance situations.

sentences.jp: <ruby>寒<rt>さむ</rt></ruby>いから、はやく<ruby>家<rt>うち</rt></ruby>に<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がって。
sentences.translation: It's cold, so come on in (the house) quickly.
sentences.notes: This shows <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる meaning to enter a home from outside — a very common everyday use distinct from just 'going up'.

sentences.jp: <ruby>最近<rt>さいきん</rt></ruby>、<ruby>物価<rt>ぶっか</rt></ruby>がどんどん<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がってるよね。
sentences.translation: Prices have been going up and up lately, haven't they.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる for rising levels or amounts — a very natural collocation with <ruby>物価<rt>ぶっか</rt></ruby>, <ruby>気温<rt>きおん</rt></ruby>, <ruby>成績<rt>せいせき</rt></ruby>, etc.

sentences.jp: <ruby>本番<rt>ほんばん</rt></ruby>になると、どうしても<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がっちゃうんだよな。
sentences.translation: When it's the real thing, I just end up getting nervous no matter what.
sentences.notes: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる meaning to freeze up or get stage fright — common in spoken Japanese when talking about performances, speeches, or exams.

sentences.jp: <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>がようやく<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がったので、<ruby>公園<rt>こうえん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>きましょう。
sentences.translation: The rain has finally let up, so let's go to the park.
sentences.notes: <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is a set phrase meaning the rain stops — this sense (something finishing or ceasing) is worth memorizing as its own chunk.

sentences.jp: <ruby>新入社員<rt>しんにゅうしゃいん</rt></ruby>の<ruby>研修<rt>けんしゅう</rt></ruby>が<ruby>無事<rt>ぶじ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がりました。
sentences.translation: The new-employee training has been successfully completed.
sentences.notes: In formal or written contexts, <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる can express that something has been finished or wrapped up — similar to <ruby>終<rt>お</rt></ruby>わる but with a nuance of successful completion.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる when you mean to physically raise or lift something — that requires the transitive <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>げる. Also avoid using <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる for elevators or escalators going up in formal signage contexts, where <ruby>上昇<rt>じょうしょう</rt></ruby> or <ruby>昇<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る may appear instead. The 'getting nervous' sense is casual spoken Japanese — don't write it in formal essays.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is saying <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がった to mean 'I climbed the mountain' — this sounds unnatural. <ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>る implies deliberate physical climbing effort (stairs, mountains, trees), while <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる focuses on the result of reaching a higher point or a more general rise. Say <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>に<ruby>登<rt>のぼ</rt></ruby>った for climbing a mountain, but <ruby>成績<rt>せいせき</rt></ruby>が<ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がった for grades going up.

frequency: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる is extremely common in daily speech across all registers. The 'enter a home' and 'prices rise' senses appear constantly in conversation; the 'get nervous' sense is frequent in casual spoken Japanese. It is one of the most versatile verbs in the language.
anki_hint: <ruby>上<rt>あ</rt></ruby>がる has four main flavors: go up physically, enter a space (like a <ruby>家<rt>うち</rt></ruby>), a level rises (<ruby>物価<rt>ぶっか</rt></ruby>・<ruby>成績<rt>せいせき</rt></ruby>), or get stage fright — picture an arrow pointing up in each situation.
